skeptic.top_risk: Per-user isolation hinges on an unspecified OAuth-identity-to-internal-user mapping and an unspecified read source (prod vs replica vs export); the six-item checklist creates false completeness around the one hard part.
skeptic.cheapest_evidence: A staging spike proving the Discord-user-id to stats-row mapping is correct and non-spoofable (two-account IDOR test) plus an explicit replica/export-vs-prod decision, before building the service.

builder.top_risk: Per-user isolation and the read-only guarantee must live in session identity + DB role, not app code — the classic IDOR/theater failure.
builder.cheapest_evidence: A one-endpoint vertical spike (GET /me/stats over a read-only DB role) with two written adversarial tests: cross-user fetch (IDOR) and missing/forged state param (CSRF).

user_value.top_risk: Broken per-user data isolation (IDOR) turns a vanity stats page into a production-data leak — the largest attack surface of the three phases for demand that is not measured.
user_value.cheapest_evidence: A written threat model plus an identify-scope-only auth spike (one endpoint returning only the logged-in user's own stats) tested against the stated abuse cases before building the versioned contract.

synth.top_risk: Per-user data isolation (IDOR): the claim's two novel, load-bearing pieces — a non-spoofable Discord-identity-to-internal-stats-row mapping bound to the server session (never client-supplied), and a genuine read-only guarantee enforced at the DB layer (replica or restricted role, not the bot's normal creds) — are asserted, not demonstrated. A single cross-user read turns a vanity page into a production-data leak. The six-item checklist front-loads well-solved OAuth/session hygiene and creates false completeness around exactly these two unmeasured items, including the unspecified read source (prod vs replica vs snapshot).
synth.cheapest_evidence: Write the abuse-case walkthrough / threat model first (required deliverable, prose only, centered on identity-mapping and IDOR), then a single-endpoint vertical spike in staging: GET /me/stats returning ONLY the session-bound user's own aggregate stats, served over a DB-level read-only role that denies writes, identify-scope only, short-TTL session cookie with no persisted token. Prove it with two written adversarial tests — cross-user fetch (account A reads B) and missing/forged state parameter (CSRF) — and make an explicit replica/snapshot-vs-prod read-source decision before building the versioned contract. Optionally sample existing !stats command usage for demand.
synth.report: Two of three probes land on HOLD; the lone BUILD is itself conditional ('resolve the read-only-role question before writing the endpoint'), so on the merits the panel is effectively unanimous that the trust gate is asserted rather than passed. Not KILL — the feature is legitimate, the design is broadly sound, and Phases 1-2 (zero auth surface) should proceed now. Not BUILD — the core claim ('buildable-as-designed AND trustworthy under adversarial verification') stakes everything on two items the checklist under-weights: (1) identity-to-data mapping — if the endpoint reads a client-supplied user id instead of the session-bound id, a perfect state parameter buys nothing and you have IDOR; (2) the read-only / read-source guarantee — 'separate service' is architecture theater unless read-only is enforced by a DB role or replica; on the bot's normal credentials, read-only is one bug away from a write, and the prod-vs-replica-vs-snapshot choice (unmeasured) dominates the trust story. Scope tension flagged by all three: the 'guilds' scope returns the user's entire server list, materially broader than needed and answerable bot-side; identify-only likely suffices and should be the default. Value/opportunity-cost lens: this is the most complex phase and the largest, permanent, public attack surface into production-derived data, for demand that was never measured, while Phases 1-2 deliver most of the value at near-zero risk. Falsifier: a single successful cross-user read (A reads B). Sequence: ship Phases 1-2 now; capture a demand signal; green-light Phase 3 only after the threat model, the identify-scope IDOR/CSRF spike over a proven read-only role, and an explicit read-source decision clear the gate. Versioned contract reads as premature unless multiple consumers are actually planned — defer it behind the spike. Smallest viable slice: identify-scope OAuth -> short-TTL server-side session (no stored token) -> one endpoint serving the caller's own XP+coins for one server over a read-only DB role with a validated state parameter, in private beta.
